They're even fund one.

And, you know, the winners are going to emerge.
And so, I think this hopes brings eternal dynamic.
And such a long time period of gestation before the companies get realized that
I don't think that will change that dynamic unfortunately.
There's also been this really interesting effect where it's been this industrialization
of venture capital, I will call it.
So, if you look at the organization that end recent has built the organization
that General Catalysis built, it's about this girthiness, right?
Across many different things, how has Sequoia reacted when they've seen those movements?
And I'm sure you've had to sit down as a partnership and say, are we matching this?
Are we copying this? Are we going to do the same thing? Are we doing something different?
That's a great question.
The industry has changed a lot since I got into venture just over 20 years ago.
If you go back to the proverbial 1990s venture firm, it was, you know, a dozen people sitting around
at a table making investment decisions with very lightweight stuff.
And it was much more of a cottage industry.
I think the industry is professionalised.
And really the founders of the ones who benefited from it, because all these firms have built larger operating teams
to be able to help those founders with talent, with go to market.
And so, I think it's really helping founders.
That's probably the main takeaway I have from that.
We've decided to not build as big an organization.
Most of the operating teams we have at Sequoia help us.
So we have about as many developers at Sequoia as we have investors.
And they're building products for us.
So that we are much more effective and productive than we might have been.
What's an example of that that they're building for you?
So, you know, on my phone, I can pull up an app that I, if you give me any company name, I'll be able to tell you who my team last
made it, how we rated it, I'll give you data on what's happening with it, hiring, how many vouched employees
they have, how do we think they're engineering team is based on their history,
they're academic profiles, etc.
All this information is that my fingertips.
If we get business plan submissions, we have an AI system that will summarize it for me,
so I get a very quick read on the company, a quick summarization of the quality of the team,
and a very quick analysis of the competitive dynamics and the other companies I should consider alongside them.
So these are just small examples of the things we do.
We just had Josiah on.
We're talking a bit about the relationship between American China.
You had a fabulous business in China for two decades, I believe, with Neil.
And it did absolutely fantastic, but then the government of the United States said,
hey, we cannot, as venture capitalists, invest in China anymore.
So what are your, what's your take on the, the opportunity in China will that return and, and just,
the, the experience you had with all those incredible hits at the time.
When we first went into China, it was 2007, I think.
The world was flat.
It was the monocrypted time.
China gained admission to the World Trade Organization in 2001.
And we will believe that it would integrate into the global economy.
That premise proved wrong.
And so we had a period where it was really interesting to be able to share knowledge and share ideas and sort of figure out how we can build a globally interconnected set of systems and companies.
And life just got too hard for that, honestly.
And we saw just more division between the two countries.
We embarked on global separation just over two years ago.
And what used to be Sequoia China is not independent business called Hongshan.
And they're off to the races.
I think this is a real challenge in China right now.
Some static are recently in 2018.
There was 51,000 companies started in China.
You had a 98% reduction in the number of companies founded in the United States.
And you were a number of companies founded in China.
Because if you're an entrepreneur in China, why would you want to start a company when the government regulations are so uncertain?
Which by the way is an interesting warning sign for me for us in America.
We think about AI policy and AI regulation.
The more uncertain to be create for founders, the more difficult it is for them to actually take that risk.
Take that leap to start a business.
But Chinese entrepreneurship is still strong.
So you see many Chinese entrepreneurs not operating in Latin America.
They move to Singapore.
They're in Japan.
They're moving to Europe.
I mean, you can't reprace that spirit.
There's an interesting dynamic that I observed, which is you have the early early stage venture companies.
Who have had an incredible track record.
You guys benchmark, co-slaw.
And then what happens is you have these late stage firms.
But many of the companies that they fund need so much money that the late stage firms can't service them.
So you have to go direct.
You go right to Saudi.
You go right to the Qataris.
You go right to the Emirates.
You go right to Norway.
You know, these sovereign wealth funds that are writing these big checks.
And so it creates this really weird dynamic where you almost become this kind of glorified placement agent almost.
So there's this part of the curve.
And then there's all this money that goes over here.
How do you adapt the business in the face of that dynamic?
Well, we stick to our knitting.
The funds we operate today are seed venture growth funds today on no bigger than they were five, six, seven years ago.
We realize that there's money to be made for some people writing very large checks and very late stage companies.
But our aspiration is to be the number one investment manager for our limited partners.
We literally want to be the best net IRR and net multiple for our LPs.
And we're not interested in maximizing fees or maximizing share of industry value creation.
That's the game we've chosen to play.
And so there's no path where Sequoia, for example, tries to go public or take the, you know, that's like it's just not in the strategy of the business.
No, actually we've structured ourselves to be a private partnership and perpetuity to the extent possible on the California law.
We have the sense of stewardship of Sequoia.
You have to leave the partnership in a better place than you found it.
Don Valentine didn't call it Valentine ventures when he started it.
He handed the partnership over to an ex-generation with Mike Marritz and Doug Leoni and Jim Gates.
And you know, we're not part of a third generation, our team currently running the partnership.
We didn't have to pay to give the partnership from the previous generation and no will be charged in the next generation.
That's our motto.
How would you describe the culture that's driven the success of the law?
So when you select partners, what do you look for?
How do you value those partners?
How do you assess the performance of those partners and how do you guys operate that kind of defines the culture?
So the most important characteristic we look for is an insatiable curiosity.
And then individual.
We look for people who are extremely driven, but they need to have a heart of gold.
So one of the things we talk about at Sequoia is we cherish individualism and teamwork.
You need an individual to be able to have a keen insight and propose an investment.
But you've got to work with a team that the whole team will be aspect is really important for us.
So we'll make investment decisions at Sequoia.
It's a consensus decision, which blew my mind when I first got there.
It's a good meaning everybody has to agree.
Everybody has to agree.
So if one person says no, it doesn't happen.
So one person can veto an investment.
And that happens often or it has.
It was a good decision and sometimes not.
What's the worst thing somebody killed?
You would call it your anti-portfolio.
It's in this case.
It wasn't just an anti-portfolio.
It's like everyone agreed.
You think about that responsibility.
And it weighs on people.
But it means that you need to show up with your best game every single day.
And you know, part of what we've done is we look at the vote distribution these days.
And look, if somebody shows up and everybody's really positive.
There are bunch of people that are eight lines out of ten.
And there's one person who clearly work up in a bad mood and there's a three.
You know, at some point that person will probably say, listen, maybe I just don't get it.
This actually happened to me.
We listen to a company in a late November.
This company is thriving right now.
They're in the, it's pretty sacks isn't here because this company's benefiting from stablecoins.
But the genius actually helped put in place.
It's really benefiting from that.
And I didn't quite get this company at the time.
And I was the only person who was below the line.
And I said, listen, there's something I'm missing on this particular company.
I think we should proceed with the investment.
Even though mine tuition working in was that we shouldn't.
And I'm really glad that we proceeded.
So can you tell us about the holding company transition that you underwent and the role of being a venture capitalist in making an exit decision?
So we did this conversation on the show a few weeks ago.
Pulled up some analysis.
The biggest winners continue to compound it's a company.

I'm assuming things would look a little bit different.
It would, yes.
So we backed a bunch of very interesting companies over 50 years.
The companies in which we were private investors when they were little companies.
Today account for over 30% of the total value of the Nasdaq.
There's no 30% of the 30% of the combined value of the Nasdaq Apple.
Apple Cisco and video, Apple, Apple, Palo Alto and A2Ox Service now.
I mean the list goes on.
So it's pretty good.
So one of the things we realized that what you're leading to is in 2022 we launched something called the Sequoia capital fund.
And see we realized that the great companies continue to compounders.
You talked about it in that episode.
It was an excellent episode obviously.
And by the way, even in more recent memory, if you look at the last 10-ish years, Palo Alto service now,
HubSpot MongoDB, these companies have all been 10-exers as public companies.
And see we've realized that when we distribute shares prematurely to LPs,
they don't know any better because they run a big endowment.
So we've decided to do is for the companies that we believe have the ability to compound longer term.
We have a different fund structure.
And so, 612, 18 months after the IPO, we can move those shares into this fund, called the Sequoia capital fund.
And this now becomes the vehicle through which we fund all our next underlying investment vehicles.
Now to give you a sense, since we launched this three and a half years ago, we've accumulated another 6.7 billion in gains.
By doing nothing except being patient.
6.7 billion dollars in gains that our LPs would not have seen if we had just distributed those shares outright.
Yeah, you're making it in the own. He's moaning.
I like it. I'm getting more and more.
But what is the sort of counter argument is, as a public company, you as venture capitalists,
who are excellent at interrogating early-stage technology, early-stage metrics, founder personalities,
all the things that might make a good venture capital investor.
Maybe as a public company, quarter to quarter, are they growing 12% or 14%.
There's a different analytical skill set, some might argue, like, you know, that belongs in that investment domain,
and frankly, it's very hard to beat the indices doing that.
What's the argument to be made to the counter and why would you counter that argument?
So, I see one of the things for us is that, I mean, in the most of these cases,
we're involved in these companies' literary ad inception.
When Palat Alternate Works was incubated inside our office with one founder and my partner Jim Gitts.
So, we've known these companies since the earliest of days, why should that relationship end at the IPO?
And in most of these cases, you just pointed out the founders are still there, and there's so much more innovation taking place.
Jack Dorsey's, one of Jack Dorsey's favorite quotes to me was,
companies have multiple founding moments.
And so, when you're in a company where the founder keeps reinventing the business,
you know, a company like Square, half the revenue today comes from a protocol cash app that hadn't launched for the first five years in the company's life.
And so, if you can find these companies, the special companies with the founders keep pushing the boundary and innovation,
and they are relentless, then it works.
Google buying YouTube.
Don't remind me.
Do you think you oughta been the same?
Would YouTube still have had the same outcome if it wasn't acquired?
And it should be clear, rule off-road in his first year or two at the company,
the DL memo to invest in YouTube.
And it got bought for $1.6 billion.
One was the same.
One business today would be worth $400,500 billion.
And then Google then invested quite significantly in infrastructure and enabling scalability and building out a team,
building out an ad revenue system.
That's that we've got Neil here tomorrow to talk about the current state of YouTube.
But do you think it could have taken the same, it's hard to say?
I think a lot of credit should go to Google for the way that's managed YouTube after that position,
both in the resources that provided the leadership and they've been able to thrive.
So one of my favorite things a Peter Tiel says is,
when an acquisition like this happens, one side was orders of magnitude off.
Like it was just a zero or it was a hundred more than what they paid.
Something is always off anyway.
Don Valentine the founder of the firm drew a four quadrant chart at one point to explain the founders that perform extremely well in terms of returns.
Maybe you could explain that to the audience.
Don pulled me aside in the early days when I joined Sequoia and he said,
two by two matrix, people are exceptional, not exceptional, easy to get along with, not so easy to get along with.
Reloaf, we normally make money in one of those four quadrants.
Your job is to figure out which one.
And it's the exceptional people who are doing lots of things.
You think it's a lot.
So then let's talk about the next generation.
So can we just double click on that? Why do you think that is?
These people change the world that aren't taken no for an answer, right?
Why does that make them hard to get along with person?
Well, I think he was saying that a little bit of a tongue-in-cheek, right?
This is a guy who backed Steve Jobs when Steve would walk around San Hill Road without shoes.
He'd come back from a trip to India, allegedly he didn't smile to great.
And he was unusual and nobody wanted to back him and, you know, don wanted to find these underdogs, these unknown...
Well, apparently some of the Atari board meetings took place in hot-ups.
But anyway, that's how he got to Steve because Steve had worked at Atari.
That's how Don got the introduction to Steve.
So I think part of the point he was trying to make is don't look for the people that, you know, we do all the right schools and we have to try to...
...and all the conventional stuff.
Founders are unconventional. These people change the world.
I mean, most of us encounter challenges every single day and we accommodate.
You know, this thing isn't quite you liking you adapt.
Founders don't. Founders think things are going, hmm, I think the world can look different.
And then they go and try to fix it.
They just don't take no for an answer.
Let's fast forward to your two mentors, Michael Moritz and Doug Leoni, two very different characters.
When the story gets told, maybe a rivalry there, between the two of them, what did you learn from each?
Sure. From Doug I learned hard.
Doug has an incredible heart.
When did you see that most? What was the moment that's coming to your mind right now that you probably shouldn't talk about?
Well, I'll give you two examples. One was in 2009 when I was in a funk. I nearly quit the business.
I didn't know this really.
But we'd had the YouTubers of great success and I felt very good about that.
And then you, in venture, after a few years, you walked through the valley of despair.
And you started to realize the things you should've invested in that you didn't.
And the lemon started to drop. The things that you did invest in that are not working out.
So yes, there was the one great quick exit but then a bunch of other things.
And I was really having a lot of self-doubt.
And Doug showed up at my house with a homemade pasteo jar.
And he didn't need to. He was in the Saturday afternoon in Oxford, my door. Who's this person at my house?
And he just wanted to tell me that he was there to support me through this dark period.
That was one example. Another one was when my son was in hospital.
And Doug showed up.
Didn't have to. That made a lot to me.
Michael just has an unbelievable ability to imagine how a company can succeed.
And when I thought about my mistakes as an investor, by the way, every single time it comes down to a failure of imagination.
That I didn't think big enough. I didn't think about how this company could progress from where they were.
You first introduced me to Twitter.
In 2007, I have the emails before smartphones launched.
And it was an ASMR app. I got tired of getting all your M. having kept a chinomacages on Twitter at the time.
I remember on your blog.
And I didn't quite imagine that it could be what it is today.
And so that to me was an example.
I remember an early meeting with Yelp.
With Jeremy Stoppelman and Max Lepchin in our offices.
And Yelp hadn't launched a web app that we're going to be an email newsletter thing.
And Michael in this meeting said, I imagine that one day, restaurants will put a Yelp sticker in the window, just like as a gut or a mission and star.
And he saw that.
He saw that. He saw that. I mean, ten years before that became a reality.
He had the imagination to think about that. That to me is amazing.
So now, Doug is still there. Mike has transitioned out. Is that what is?
Yes. Michael has transitioned on completely.
Doug has stepped back from day to day in B.S.
So he's no longer an important meeting in S.R.T.M.
He's engaged to serve on several boards.
And you're in charge.
I'm the leader of a team.
I think it's more like being captain of the team.
We play teams for it. We play teams for it.
And we are equal partners.
Was it hard to see those two guys go to seeing how legendary they were?
To succeed them?
Well, both to succeed them.
But also to see them walk out the door like as your attendance.
You want to keep them around as long as possible.
Sounds like a stand-alone question.
Well, firstly, it is very hard to succeed them.
I think every single person that's a quite full.
This enormous burden and responsibility to try to match the performance that we've been known for.
But we have this great generational transition that's a quite.
So Michael stepped back from day to day activity in 2012.
And he was...
Marks that in 2012, Michael stepped back.
He had personal health reasons and he stepped back.
He continued to serve on the boards that he was on.
And we had him from another decade where I would ask him to advise.
As we were going through global separation that we talked about earlier,
I would ask, Doug and Michael, what do you think should we...
What should we be doing here? Do you have advice for me?
And so, it's a way we have this benefit of intergenerational knowledge transfer.
Doug was on a call earlier today actually.
We had a difficult conversation.
I wanted his input on an important question.
And it's not because he has the authority to tell me what to do.
It's because I seek out his advice.
You were investing in traditional software, internet services for years.
And then a couple of years ago, you started investing in some life sciences.
There's life sciences kind of work as a venture investment today.
And what's been the challenge in biotech and life sciences investing generally over the last few years.
There's a lot of notoriety about the collapse of the market.
And even Dave Ricks today was saying,
Most public biotech companies are trading below cash.
What's your observations on the business model?
What you've seen the types of businesses even invested in there?
So the business we invested in that has done really well as a company called Natira.
And we made a seed investment of a million dollars in 2007 in this company.
20 billion now, right?
20 billion dollar market capital.
There was a million dollars.
Two people with a very raw idea.
And today they're the leading provider of prenatal testing on ecology, recurrent,
spawn and train organ transplant projection testing.
And so that company's been a huge success.
Diagnostics, genetic diagnostics.
This is been a huge success.
And you think about the dividend we still collecting from the human genome project 25 years ago.
And you've obviously seen that in some of the businesses that you've helped build as well.
We did make an investment in a comical bridge bio, which is helping with
regenerative disease, drug development.
But other than that, I think we just don't have the expertise for biotech.
You know, we don't have any image.
There are still winners.
They are still winners.
But we have no MDPHDs on our team in Sequoia.
And I think, you know, it's very dangerous when people think that your success in one
domain just naturally makes you give you the right to compete in other domains.
I think I have tremendous respect for the people who understand that.
I do not.
I learned that one the hard way.
Ladies and gentlemen, roll out a lot better.
Thanks, ladies and gentlemen.
Thank you, sir.
I appreciate you coming out.
